text: The laparoscopic procedure begins with the surgeon cutting the left colic flexure. The greater omentum is incised to access the omental bursa, revealing the liver and pancreas. Varicose short gastric arteries from the splenic artery are visible in the middle of the frame. The pancreatic serosa is opened.

The surgeon identifies the splenic artery above the pancreas and dissects under the pancreas to reveal the splenic vein. An ultrasound probe is used to examine the pancreas for tumor-free tissue at the planned resection site. The splenic artery is cut and ligated using an endogia stapler, which places two triple rows of staples and cuts the tissue between them. The splenic vein is similarly cut and ligated.

The surgeon uses a larger version of the endo-GIA to divide the pancreas in tumor-free tissue, with the distal end containing the tumor visible on the right side of the frame. Varicose arteries are cut using the endo-GIA. The stomach is visible on the left side of the frame. The spleen's attachment to the diaphragm is dissected, revealing inflammatory adhesions and a poorly defined division between the stomach and spleen against the diaphragm. After a challenging dissection, the stomach is separated from the spleen and cut with the endo-GIA.

The surgeon cuts the specimen loose and places it in an endo-catch bag, extracting it through an extended umbilical incision. The staple sutures are visible on the remaining pancreas and stomach, with the liver positioned at the top of the frame. A TACO cell surgical patch covers the remaining pancreas.
The surgeon places a drain before closing the surgical wounds. The pancreas is cut open, revealing a solid yellowish tumor within the tissue.
